title: Public Works and Infrastructure Committee - April 23, 2026
meeting_location: Champlain Room, 110 Laurier Avenue West, and by electronic participation

agenda_items.title: Notices and meeting information for meeting participants and the public

Public Notices French - Hybrid Standing Committee 2025 (AODA).pdf

agenda_items.description:
Notices and meeting information are attached to the agenda and minutes, including: availability of simultaneous interpretation and accessibility accommodations; in camera meeting procedures; information items not subject to discussion; personal information disclaimer for correspondents and public speakers; notices regarding minutes; and hybrid participation details.
Accessible formats and communication supports are available, upon request.
Except where otherwise indicated, reports requiring Council consideration will be presented to Council on Wednesday, May 13, 2026 in Public Works and Infrastructure Committee Report 26.
The deadline to register by phone to speak, or submit written comments or visual presentations is 4 pm on Wednesday, April 22, 2026, and the deadline to register to speak by email is 8:30 am on Thursday, April 23, 2026.
agenda_items.minutes: These “Summary Minutes” indicate the disposition of items and actions taken at the meeting. This document does not include all of the text that will be included in the full Minutes, such as the record of written and oral submissions. Recorded votes and dissents contained in the Summary Minutes are draft until the full Minutes of the meeting are confirmed by the Committee. The draft of the full Minutes (for confirmation) will be published with the agenda for the next regular Committee meeting and, once confirmed, will replace this document.

sub_agendas_items.title: Implications of Bill 56 and Strategic Road Safety Action Plan Annual Report

attachments.attachment_title: English Report - Implications of Bill 56 and Strategic Road Safety Action Plan Annual Report (REV).pdf
French Report - Implications of Bill 56 and Strategic Road Safety Action Plan Annual Report (REV).pdf

sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Heidi Cousineau, Manager, Traffic, Safety & Mobility, Public Works Department, Svetlana Valkova, Deputy CFO, Financial Services, Finance and Corporate Services Department & Lance Nowak, Manager, Strategic Projects, Finance and Corporate Services Department provided a presentation on the item and answered questions from the Committee. A copy of the slide presentation is filed with the Office of the City Clerk.
Krista Tanaka, Director, Traffic Services, Public Works Department, Cathy Kourouma, Program Manager, Road Safety, Public Works Department, Taffy Nahas, Legal Counsel, Legal Services and Martin Groulx, Inspector, Ottawa Police Services also answered questions from the Committee regarding the report.
Additionally, the following written correspondence was received by and filed with the Office of the City Clerk and distributed to Committee Members:
Lastly, motions were introduced and voted on by the Committee. The motions were 'CARRIED' and the item was 'CARRIED AS AMENDED'.

motions.motion_text:
Report Recommendation(s)
That Public Works and Infrastructure Committee recommend that Council:

WHEREAS speeding continues to be one of the most significant road‑safety concerns raised by residents across the City of Ottawa, particularly in residential neighbourhoods and school zones where vulnerable road users, including children, are at heightened risk; and
WHEREAS recent City data has shown a substantial increase in speeding in school zones, with compliance dropping from 87 percent to 41 percent within a 12‑week period following the removal of Automated Speed Enforcement (ASE) cameras, and high‑end speeding increasing from 0.3 percent to over 4 percent during the same period; and
WHEREAS speed data is an important input in understanding risk related to more serious collisions; and
WHEREAS the city’s current approach to collecting speed data is limited and does not provide a full picture of speeds across the roadway network, and
WHEREAS other jurisdictions across Canada and internationally are increasingly incorporating innovative, technology‑enabled, and data‑driven approaches—including, predictive analytics, and AI‑supported monitoring systems—to inform their road safety programs; and
WHEREAS the City of Ottawa is currently undertaking work to update the Road Safety Action Plan, which will guide the road safety priorities for the next term of Council; and
WHEREAS this work presents an opportunity to modernize and strengthen the inputs used to make informed data-driven decisions about road safety;
THERFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT staff, through the update to the City’s Road Safety Action Plan, leverage advanced data analytics, predictive modelling, and AI‑supported technologies, where appropriate, to enhance the City’s ability to identify, monitor, and respond to speeding trends and inform road safety priorities
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED THAT staff consider speed management as a focus area when developing the Road Safety Action Plan that will be presented to Council in 2027.

sub_agendas_items.title: Update on Ottawa Gatineau Bike Share Feasibility Study

Document 2 - Executive Summary Ottawa-Gatineau Bike Share Feasibility Study - English.pdf
Document 2 - Sommaires exécutif - Étude de faisabilité vélopartage Ottawa-Gatineau - French.pdf
Document 3 - Ottawa-Gatineau Bike Share Feasibility Study - English.pdf
Document 3 - Ottawa-Gatineau Bike Share Feasibility Study - French.pdf

sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Within the will of Committee, the item was lifted for discussion.
Jennifer Armstrong, Director, Transportation Planning, Planning, Development & Building Department, Marcia Wallace, General Manager, Planning, Development Building Services, Alain Gonthier, General Manager, Public Works Department, Scott Caldwell, Manager, Parking Services, Public Works Department & Kalle Hakala, Program Manager, Active Transportation Planning, Planning, Development & Building Department answered questions from Committee.
The following residents spoke before the Committee and provided comments on the item:
Additionally, written correspondence from Xavier Sythe dated April 22, 2026 was received by and filed with the Office of the City Clerk and distributed to Committee Members.
After the discussion, a motion from Councillor S. Devine (on behalf of S. Menard) was introduced. A recorded vote on the first "THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED" clause was requested and a Point of Order with regards to the rationale to remove the said clause was raised by Councillor S. Devine. The Chair ruled it by re-calling for the recorded vote, that resulted on striking it out from the motion text.
Lastly, the motion was then 'CARRIED' by the Committee.

motions.motion_text:
WHEREAS Bike Share systems increase transit connectivity, mobility options for residents, and support the City’s efforts to encourage cycling as a space-efficient, healthy, sustainable mode of travel; and
WHEREAS since 2023 there is new information about bike share that is relevant for Ottawa, including the Bike Share in Ottawa-Gatineau Initial Report, Transportation Association of Canada report on Shared Micromobility Services in Canadian Communities, and the Ottawa-Gatineau Bike Share Feasibility Study; and
WHEREAS staff have prepared a memo to the Public Works and Infrastructure Committee that summarizes the new information; and
WHEREAS the Feasibility Study suggests pursuing a publicly supported bike share system using docked bikes for an initial roll-out, and could offer a number of advantages in terms of fleet deployment, parking, program continuity and user affordability; and
WHEREAS staff will benefit from taking the time to assess options and potential partnership opportunities to present to the next term of Council;
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that as part of this work, staff pursue funding discussions with the National Capital Commission and identify possible grant opportunities, in order to reduce costs to the municipality; and
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED that staff report back to Public Works and Infrastructure Committee by Q1 2027 with recommendations for implementing a bike share system in Ottawa, to enable a bike share system to be launched as early as possible in the next Term of Council, subject to Council approval.

THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED that staff be directed to develop a capital and operating funding strategy for a bike share system in Ottawa, considering the findings from the Feasibility Study and the results of the additional assessment; and

agenda_items.title: Notices of Motions (For Consideration at Subsequent Meeting)

motions.motion_text:
WHEREAS the intersections of Solarium Avenue and Andromeda Road, Brian Good Avenue and Chorus Drive, and Kelly Farm Drive and Sora Way are located within growing residential communities with increasing pedestrian, cyclist, and vehicular activity; and
WHEREAS residents have raised ongoing concerns regarding traffic safety, pedestrian crossings, and vehicle speeds at these locations; and
WHEREAS staff have reviewed these locations and determined that they do not currently meet the Council-approved warrants for the installation of an all-way stop control; and
WHEREAS the installation of an all-way stop control at the intersections of Solarium Avenue and Andromeda Road, and Brian Good Avenue and Chorus Drive would trigger the Integrated Accessibility Standards Regulation (IASR) under theAccessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act, 2005(AODA), requiring curb depressions aligned with pedestrian travel path and properly placed tactile walking surface indicators (TWSIs); and
WHEREAS the intersection of Kelly Farm Drive and Sora Way meets applicable accessibility requirements and can accommodate an all-way stop control through the installation of signage and pavement markings only; and
WHEREAS staff, in coordination with the applicable developer/contractor, are prepared to implement the required works at the intersections of Solarium Avenue and Andromeda Road, and Brian Good Avenue and Chorus Drive, with funding to be provided through existing accounts and, if necessary, the Ward Councillor’s Temporary Traffic Calming budget;
THEREFORE BE IT RESOLVED THAT the Public Works and Infrastructure Committee approve the installation of all-way stop controls at the following intersections:
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED THAT the implementation of all-way stop controls at Solarium Avenue and Andromeda Road, and Brian Good Avenue and Chorus Drive, be subject to completion of all required accessibility upgrades to ensure compliance with theAccessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act, 2005(AODA); and
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED THAT  staff coordinate the installation of permanent stop control signage at the intersections of Solarium Avenue and Andromeda Road, and at Brian Good Avenue and Chorus Drive, to be funded through Transportation Planning budgets, and if additional funding is required, through the Ward Councillor’s Temporary Traffic Calming budget; and
BE IT FURTHER RESOLVED THAT staff be directed to install temporary stop control signage at the intersection of Kelly Farm Drive and Sora Way immediately as an interim measure, pending installation of permanent signage and pavement markings, to be installed through Traffic Services’ operating budget.

sub_agendas_items.title: Roadway Signage – Posts
sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Submitted by Councillor W. Lo
Signage is likely the most visible feature of our roadways, but unfortunately, it’s quite normal to see signposts bent, twisted, or destroyed when travelling across the City. Although that issue is mostly cosmetic, it sometimes results in missing signage, such as hazard markers and other vital information for motorists. Based on resident feedback and personal observations, it is almost always U-channel signposts which are in a damaged state resulting from wind, snow being pushed, and other non-vehicle contact incidents. This despite other types of signposts used by the city, such as tubular posts used for street name signage (and occasionally for regular signage) and wooden posts withstanding the same variables. Could staff kindly provide the following information:

sub_agendas_items.title: Overhead Street Name Signs
sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Submitted by Councillor W. Lo
When updated overhead street name signs were first introduced almost three years ago, an error resulted in the City’s corporate branding being left off the signage. The error was corrected and all new overhead street name signs are now branded appropriately. At the time, City staff noted the branding is required by policy. While a municipality may have its own design for street name signage (including unique designs for special districts), including branding is not common practice, and likely requires additional material and labour. Could staff kindly provide the following information:

sub_agendas_items.title: Reduce vehicle speeds
sub_agendas_items.minutes:
Submitted by Councillor L. Johnson
The City has two programs aimed at making road design changes to reduce vehicle speeds: the Neighbourhood Traffic Calming (NTC) program, which is within the Planning Department; and the Temporary Traffic Calming (TTC) program, which is within the Traffic section of Public Works.
​There have been times when the two programs seem to overlap, or where it’s difficult to tell which one should have ownership of a situation. Furthermore, some TTC measures – such as speed humps – are actually permanent traffic calming measures; and increasingly we see TTC measures reinstalled year after year at increasing costs. 
In 2023, I asked staff whether it was time to review how these programs interact and whether they needed to be rationalized in some way. 
In June, 2024, several councillors, including myself, were interviewed by staff about the two programs and how they might better interact. We continue to await the promised service coordination and residents continue to be concerned about speeding, serious injury, and death.
